squared test) greater prevalence of osteoarthritis in cap-
tive animals. No wild-caught individuals demonstrated 
bony changes consistent with osteoarthritis. This finding 
is significant for the population of pet guinea pigs. The 
effect of captivity is also highlighted by the research of 
Wallace et al. (2019), which found a greater severity of 
osteoarthritis in guinea pigs whose activity had been 
forcibly restricted compared with those given access to 
space and the ability to exercise.
In humans, osteoarthritis is recognized as a significant 
cause of chronic pain and disability among adults. Given 
the high incidence of osteoarthritis in older guinea pigs, 
it is likely that it is also a cause of pain and disability in a 
large proportion of these animals. It is also likely that it is 
underdiagnosed. The long-term consequences of osteo-
arthritis include weight gain, muscle weakness and 
increased risk of injury due to reduced mobility. It is pos-
sible that problems such as pododermatitis, perineal soil-
ing/anal impaction, urolithiasis, overgrown claws, spurs 
(pad hyperkeratosis) and poor grooming are caused or  24.2 Guinea pig receiving K-laser therapy for osteoarthritis.
exacerbated by osteoarthritis in guinea pigs that have this 
condition. Modifications to the animals’ husbandry and environ-
Conventional radiography is the standard diagnostic  ment can be very helpful for guinea pigs with osteoarthritis.

patients’ small size, and moderate to severe disease is  an appropriate bodyweight, may be useful (Begum-Dia-

changes are likely to correlate poorly with levels of pain. Dermatological diseases

tion and thereby causes fewer side effects, has been  soiled bedding are all significant risk factors for pododer-

shown to decrease pain in several rodent inflammatory  cardiorespiratory disorders.

(a)
24.4 Impacted rump gland in a mature boar.
(b) Dental disorders
In a survey of 1000 guinea pigs examined at a university 
veterinary hospital in the Czech Republic, Minarikova et al. 
(2015) found that 36% had acquired dental disease (ADD). 
The largest proportion of these animals was aged between 
2 and 5 years, and boars were predominantly affected. The 
severity of ADD increases with age (Jenkins, 2010) and the 
disease results in chronic pain, alterations in the mobility/
range of motion of the jaw (temporomandibular subluxation, 
with osteoarthritis in the temporomandibular joint often 
developing as a consequence), inability to eat a normal 
diet, and second-order effects such as digestive upset, 
ileus (gut stasis) and weight loss. Many cases of ADD occur 
(c)
secondary to other factors affecting the animal’s appetite;

bronchodilators such as salbutamol or theobromine.
Nebulization therapy is commonly used as an adjunct 
to systemic therapies in guinea pigs with respiratory dis-
ease. It allows medication to be applied topically to the 
nasal cavities and airways down to the alveoli. Equipment 
that can produce droplets smaller than 5 μm (ideally 
1–3 μm) should be used. While nebulizing saline alone will 
promote respiratory tract health by moisturizing the air-
ways and improving mucociliary clearance, drugs can also 
be applied by nebulization. Antibiotics, mucolytics, diuret-
ics, bronchodilators and disinfectants are all commonly 
applied in this way. The length of time for which an animal 
receives nebulization therapy is important: a period of at 
least 30 minutes is thought to be required to deliver medi-
cations to the deepest parts of the respiratory tract.
Reproductive and urinary tract disorders
Ovarian cysts are the most common reproductive disorder 
seen in adult sows (see Chapters 14 and 18). Whether these 
are clinically significant or not, approximately one-third of 
sows are found to have swellings in the area of the ovaries 
on clinical examination. Most sows will develop some form 
of ovarian cyst during their life. Nielsen et al. (2003) dem-
onstrated that both the incidence of cysts and their size  Lateral radiograph of a guinea pig with chronic tubular
